flashcards.q: What is matter?
flashcards.a: Anything that has mass and occupies space (volume). Examples: air, water, iron, wood.

flashcards.q: What are the three characteristics of particles of matter?
flashcards.a: (1) They have spaces between them. (2) They are in continuous random motion. (3) They attract each other.

flashcards.q: What is kinetic energy of particles?
flashcards.a: The energy possessed by particles due to their continuous motion. It increases with temperature — higher temperature = faster-moving particles.

flashcards.q: What is diffusion?
flashcards.a: The spontaneous intermixing of particles of two different substances due to continuous particle motion. Rate: gases > liquids > solids. Increases with temperature.

flashcards.q: What does 'interparticle space' mean? Which state has the most?
flashcards.a: The empty space between particles of matter. Gases have the maximum interparticle space; solids have the minimum.

flashcards.q: List three properties of solids.
flashcards.a: Definite shape, definite volume, negligible compressibility (rigid, maximum intermolecular forces).

flashcards.q: List three properties of liquids.
flashcards.a: No fixed shape (take shape of container), fixed volume, can flow (fluids). Intermediate intermolecular forces.

flashcards.q: List three properties of gases.
flashcards.a: No fixed shape or volume, highly compressible, exert pressure on container walls, very fast diffusion. Negligible intermolecular forces.

flashcards.q: Why are gases compressible but solids are not?
flashcards.a: Gases have large interparticle spaces — pressure pushes particles into these spaces. Solids have negligible spaces — particles are already closely packed.

flashcards.q: How does a gas exert pressure on container walls?
flashcards.a: Gas particles move randomly at high speed, repeatedly colliding with the walls. The force of these collisions per unit area = gas pressure.

flashcards.q: What is the melting point of ice?
What is the boiling point of water?
Convert: °C to K formula?
Convert: K to °C formula?
What is melting (fusion)?
flashcards.a: The change of solid state into liquid state on heating. Occurs at the melting point.

flashcards.q: What does the melting point indicate?
flashcards.a: The strength of the intermolecular forces — higher melting point = stronger forces of attraction between particles.

flashcards.q: What is latent heat? Why is it called 'latent'?
flashcards.a: Heat absorbed/released during a change of state at CONSTANT temperature. Called 'latent' (hidden) because it doesn't show as a temperature rise — it goes into breaking/forming intermolecular bonds.

flashcards.q: What is the latent heat of fusion?
flashcards.a: The heat required to convert 1 kg of solid into liquid at its melting point at atmospheric pressure, without temperature change.

flashcards.q: What is the latent heat of vaporisation?
flashcards.a: The heat required to convert 1 kg of liquid into gas at its boiling point at atmospheric pressure, without temperature change.

flashcards.q: Why does temperature remain constant during melting?
flashcards.a: All heat supplied is used to overcome intermolecular forces (latent heat of fusion), not to increase particle kinetic energy.

flashcards.q: Why do particles in steam at 373 K have more energy than water at 373 K?
flashcards.a: Steam has absorbed extra latent heat of vaporisation (~2260 J/g) to overcome intermolecular forces — stored as potential energy, not temperature.

flashcards.q: What is sublimation?
flashcards.a: Direct change of solid to gas WITHOUT passing through the liquid state. Examples: camphor, naphthalene, dry ice (solid CO₂).

flashcards.q: What is deposition?
flashcards.a: Direct change of gas to solid WITHOUT passing through the liquid state. It is the reverse of sublimation.

flashcards.q: What is dry ice? Why is it 'dry'?
flashcards.a: Solid CO₂ stored under high pressure. At 1 atm, it sublimes directly to gas — no liquid is formed, so it leaves no 'wet' residue.

flashcards.q: How can gases be liquefied?
flashcards.a: By applying high pressure (brings particles closer) AND lowering temperature (reduces kinetic energy). Both together allow intermolecular forces to hold particles as liquid.

flashcards.q: Do both temperature AND pressure determine the state of matter?
flashcards.a: Yes. Both together determine whether a substance is solid, liquid, or gas.

flashcards.q: What is evaporation?
flashcards.a: Change of liquid to vapour at any temperature BELOW its boiling point. It is a SURFACE phenomenon.

flashcards.q: Evaporation vs Boiling — key difference?
flashcards.a: Evaporation: surface phenomenon, any temperature, slow. Boiling: bulk phenomenon, only at boiling point, rapid.

flashcards.q: Name the 4 factors that increase evaporation rate.
flashcards.a: (1) Increased surface area. (2) Increased temperature. (3) Decreased humidity. (4) Increased wind speed.

flashcards.q: How does evaporation cause cooling?
flashcards.a: Evaporating particles absorb latent heat from the surroundings/body surface, lowering its temperature.

flashcards.q: Why do cotton clothes keep us cool in summer?
flashcards.a: Cotton absorbs sweat (perspiration) and exposes it over a large surface for evaporation. Evaporating sweat absorbs latent heat from the body — cooling it.

flashcards.q: Why does an earthen pot (matka) keep water cool?
flashcards.a: Water seeps through porous clay walls and evaporates from the outer surface, absorbing latent heat from the water inside, cooling it.

flashcards.q: Why is ice at 273 K more effective in cooling than water at 273 K?
flashcards.a: Ice additionally absorbs latent heat of fusion (~334 J/g) while melting, extra heat absorbed from the surroundings. Water only absorbs heat by temperature rise.

flashcards.q: Why does steam cause more severe burns than boiling water at 100°C?
flashcards.a: Steam releases latent heat of vaporisation (~2260 J/g) on condensing, in addition to the heat from cooling — much more heat transferred to skin.

flashcards.q: What is humidity? How does it affect evaporation?
flashcards.a: Humidity = amount of water vapour in air. High humidity → air nearly saturated → evaporation slows. Low humidity → air can absorb more vapour → evaporation is faster.
